prompt: Compare how Agent Lightning, AgentRL, and rLLM structure agent training pipelines.
reference_answer: Agent Lightning focuses on decoupling agent execution from RL training through a unified data interface and almost zero code modifications. AgentRL emphasizes a fully asynchronous multi-turn, multi-task training and environment stack with separate rollout, actor, and reference workers. rLLM is positioned as a reusable framework or substrate that can train many agent frameworks with near-zero code changes and multiple RL backends.

documents.title: Agent Lightning digest
documents.content: Agent Lightning is organized around complete decoupling between agent execution and training, using a unified data interface and almost zero code modifications for existing agents. Its design focuses on plugging RL into existing agent stacks without large rewrites.

documents.title: AgentRL repository digest
documents.content: AgentRL emphasizes a fully asynchronous multi-turn, multi-task training pipeline with rollout workers, actor workers, and reference workers. The framework treats asynchronous coordination as the core way to scale agentic RL updates.

documents.title: rLLM repository digest
documents.content: rLLM acts like a reusable agent-training substrate with near-zero code changes, a CLI-first workflow, and multiple RL algorithms and training backends. The project highlights framework ergonomics and compatibility with many agent stacks.

required_claims.text: Agent Lightning is organized around decoupling agent execution from training with a unified interface and minimal rewrites.
AgentRL emphasizes a fully asynchronous multi-turn, multi-task pipeline with rollout, actor, and reference workers.
rLLM acts like a reusable agent-training substrate with near-zero code changes and multiple RL backends.
The three systems differ because Agent Lightning focuses on execution-training decoupling, AgentRL on asynchronous training infrastructure, and rLLM on reusable framework ergonomics.

provenance.refresh_hint: Refresh if repository READMEs or training-stack descriptions change materially.
